lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: My lords, at once: the cause why we are met

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Is, to determine of the coronation.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: In God’s name, speak: when is the royal day?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Are all things fitting for that royal time?

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: It is, and wants but nomination.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: To-morrow, then, I judge a happy day.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Who knows the lord protector’s mind herein?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Who is most inward with the noble duke?

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Your grace, we think, should soonest know his mind.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Who, I, my lord! we know each other’s faces,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But for our hearts, he knows no more of mine,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Than I of yours;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Nor I no more of his, than you of mine.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Lord Hastings, you and he are near in love.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I thank his grace, I know he loves me well;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But, for his purpose in the coronation,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I have not sounded him, nor he deliver’d

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: His gracious pleasure any way therein:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But you, my noble lords, may name the time;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And in the duke’s behalf I’ll give my voice,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Which, I presume, he’ll take in gentle part.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Now in good time, here comes the duke himself.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My noble lords and cousins all, good morrow.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I have been long a sleeper; but, I hope,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My absence doth neglect no great designs,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which by my presence might have been concluded.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Had not you come upon your cue, my lord,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: William Lord Hastings had pronounced your part—

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I mean, your voice—for crowning of the king.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Than my Lord Hastings no man might be bolder;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His lordship knows me well, and loves me well.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I thank your grace.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My lord of Ely!

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: When I was last in Holborn,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I saw good strawberries in your garden there:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do beseech you send for some of them.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Marry, and will, my lord, with all my heart.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Cousin of Buckingham, a word with you.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Catesby hath sounded Hastings in our business,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And finds the testy gentleman so hot,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As he will lose his head ere give consent

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His master’s son, as worshipful as he terms it,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Shall lose the royalty of England’s throne.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Withdraw you hence, my lord, I’ll follow you.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: We have not yet set down this day of triumph.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: To-morrow, in mine opinion, is too sudden;

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: For I myself am not so well provided

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: As else I would be, were the day prolong’d.

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Where is my lord protector? I have sent for these strawberries.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: His grace looks cheerfully and smooth to-day;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: There’s some conceit or other likes him well,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: When he doth bid good morrow with such a spirit.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I think there’s never a man in Christendom

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: That can less hide his love or hate than he;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: For by his face straight shall you know his heart.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: What of his heart perceive you in his face

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: By any likelihood he show’d to-day?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Marry, that with no man here he is offended;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: For, were he, he had shown it in his looks.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: I pray God he be not, I say.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I pray you all, tell me what they deserve

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That do conspire my death with devilish plots

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of damned witchcraft, and that have prevail’d

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Upon my body with their hellish charms?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: The tender love I bear your grace, my lord,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Makes me most forward in this noble presence

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: To doom the offenders, whatsoever they be:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I say, my lord, they have deserved death.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Then be your eyes the witness of this ill:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: See how I am bewitch’d; behold mine arm

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Is, like a blasted sapling, wither’d up:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And this is Edward’s wife, that monstrous witch,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Consorted with that harlot strumpet Shore,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That by their witchcraft thus have marked me.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: If they have done this thing, my gracious lord—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If! thou protector of this damned strumpet,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Tellest thou me of “ifs”? Thou art a traitor:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Off with his head! Now, by Saint Paul I swear,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I will not dine until I see the same.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Lovel and Ratcliff, look that it be done:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The rest, that love me, rise and follow me.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Woe, woe for England! not a whit for me;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: For I, too fond, might have prevented this.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Stanley did dream the boar did raze his helm;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But I disdain’d it, and did scorn to fly:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Three times to-day my foot-cloth horse did stumble,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And startled, when he look’d upon the Tower,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: As loath to bear me to the slaughter-house.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: O, now I want the priest that spake to me:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I now repent I told the pursuivant,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: As ’twere triumphing at mine enemies,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: How they at Pomfret bloodily were butcher’d,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And I myself secure in grace and favour.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: O Margaret, Margaret, now thy heavy curse

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Is lighted on poor Hastings’ wretched head!

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Dispatch, my lord; the duke would be at dinner:

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Make a short shrift; he longs to see your head.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: O momentary grace of mortal men,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Which we more hunt for than the grace of God!

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Who builds his hopes in air of your good looks,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Lives like a drunken sailor on a mast,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Ready, with every nod, to tumble down

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Into the fatal bowels of the deep.

lines.speaker: LOVEL
lines.text: Come, come, dispatch; ’tis bootless to exclaim.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: O bloody Richard! miserable England!

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I prophesy the fearfull’st time to thee

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: That ever wretched age hath look’d upon.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Come, lead me to the block; bear him my head:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: They smile at me that shortly shall be dead.

stage_directions.text: Enter Buckingham, Derby, Hastings, the Bishop of Ely, Ratcliff, Lovel, with others, and take their seats at a table.
Exeunt all but Hastings, Ratcliff, and Lovel.

glosses.definition: settle the date and details of the coronation
the naming of the day
most intimate, closest
probed his opinion
arrived on your cue (theatrical metaphor — Buckingham treats the room as a stage)
Ely's Holborn strawberries — the infamous abrupt transition; Richard uses the request to exit briefly and return transformed
irritable gentleman so inflamed
brightly and pleasantly
idea, notion

glosses.phrase: by his face straight shall you know his heart
glosses.definition: Hastings' fatal misjudgement — one of the play's grimmest dramatic ironies

glosses.phrase: conspire my death with devilish plots
glosses.definition: plot my death with devilish schemes — Richard's manufactured accusation
accursed sorcery — witchcraft was a capital offence under 1563 and 1604 statutes
struck young-tree, shriveled — Richard holds up his already-deformed arm as 'evidence' of witchcraft
Jane Shore, labelled doubly; the charge is cynically fabricated
the scene's most famous line; summary execution by royal fiat
a horse with a trailing cloth of state — a noble's ceremonial mount
now I need the priest (for last rites) — Hastings in retrospect
brief confession before death — origin of the modern idiom
the fleeting favour of men — versus the grace of God
a sailor perched on a masthead — ready to tumble with any motion
useless to cry out

annotations.title: The strawberries of Ely Place, and the trap
annotations.body: Richard's apparent non sequitur — "When I was last in Holborn, / I saw good strawberries in your garden there: / I do beseech you send for some of them" — is one of Shakespeare's most quoted off-kilter moments, and it is inherited directly from Thomas More's History, where the detail is recorded verbatim as from eyewitness testimony. The Bishop of Ely's town palace in Holborn had famous strawberry gardens. Shakespeare uses the historical detail to construct a theatrical trap: Richard ambles into the council meeting cheerful and inconsequent, exits briefly (ostensibly to fetch strawberries), and returns moments later to accuse Hastings of witchcraft and order his head. The strawberries serve as cover for Richard's consultation with Buckingham — and as a signal to the audience that the council's apparent ordinariness is a stage set for murder.
sources: Thomas More, History of King Richard III (c. 1513)
John Jowett, The Oxford Shakespeare: Richard III (2000)

annotations.title: Hastings' last and worst judgement
annotations.body: Hastings' speech immediately before his accusation — "His grace looks cheerfully and smooth to-day... / For by his face straight shall you know his heart" — is one of Shakespeare's great ironies, and it registers as tragedy rather than simple dramatic irony because Hastings is not stupid. He has read Richard's face and trusted the reading. The play's claim is that this reading-method cannot work with Richard, whose face is a deliberate instrument. Hastings' confidence is the precise trap the play has been setting since 1.3. Derby's quiet counter-line — "I pray God he be not, I say" — is the only honest response Hastings' blindness permits. The audience, again, knows more than the stage: we have watched Richard give Catesby his orders about Hastings in 3.1. The scene demonstrates that political experience is no defence against Richard's mode of dissimulation.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: The withered arm: staging the impossible charge
annotations.body: Richard's production of his "blasted sapling, wither'd up" arm, blamed on Elizabeth and Jane Shore's witchcraft, enacts on stage what More reports as the scene's central spectacle. Two things are going on at once. First, everyone present knows that Richard's arm has always been as it is; the "withering" is not new. Second, Richard's point is precisely that the charge need not be plausible. What he needs is a pretext sufficient to move immediately. Hastings' "If they have done this thing, my gracious lord —" is the word that dooms him: the conditional "if" is treated as treason, because Richard has decided the accusation is not a claim but an instruction. Shakespeare is showing a show-trial in miniature. The charge is not a theory of what happened; it is a theory of what will happen, stated backward.
sources: Thomas More, History of King Richard III (c. 1513)
Stephen Greenblatt, Tyrant: Shakespeare on Politics (2018)

annotations.title: "Momentary grace of mortal men"
annotations.body: Hastings' final soliloquy — "O momentary grace of mortal men, / Which we more hunt for than the grace of God!" — is the play's first fully articulated moral retrospect. The drunken-sailor-on-a-mast image compresses the court-life critique into a seafarer's emblem: those who build their hopes on a monarch's favourable looks live without stable footing. The speech is also, structurally, an echo of Clarence's dream-speech in 1.4 — another figure about to die, pulled from confidence to drowning. Shakespeare is quietly repeating forms: the self-conscious fall-speech becomes a convention the play establishes now and exploits later. Buckingham in 5.1 and Richard himself in 5.3 will each, in different ways, deliver versions of this retrospect.
sources: Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: Richard's request for strawberries from the Bishop of Ely's garden is drawn from what source?
options: Thomas More's History of King Richard III, which reports the detail from eyewitness testimony
A forged document from the Cromwell era

trials.prompt: Hastings says of Richard "For by his face straight shall you know his heart." The line's tragic irony is that:
options: It is a deliberate lie Hastings tells to flatter Richard
Hastings' reading-method, sound in general, fails with Richard, whose face is a deliberate instrument of dissimulation
It is a mistranslation of a Latin commonplace
Hastings is referring to a different person, not Richard

trials.prompt: Richard's charge that Jane Shore and Elizabeth have withered his arm by witchcraft serves what rhetorical purpose?
options: An instruction disguised as accusation — Richard does not need the charge to be plausible, only sufficient to move against Hastings immediately
A standard legal procedure for treason trials
A ploy to gain sympathy from the council

trials.prompt: Hastings' line "O momentary grace of mortal men, / Which we more hunt for than the grace of God!" is structurally what?
options: The play's first fully articulated moral retrospect, establishing a convention the play will reuse in Buckingham's 5.1 and Richard's 5.3 speeches
A private confession meant only for his own ear
